title: Used Slot Machines vs. New: Is Buying Refurbished Worth It?
excerpt: Compare refurbished slot machines at $800-$3,500 versus new at $15,000-$25,000. Learn why casinos retire working machines and what you actually sacrifice buying used.
body:
One of the most common questions I get from first-time buyers is whether a used or refurbished slot machine is worth it compared to buying new. After 33 years in this business, my answer is always the same: for home use, refurbished is not just worth it, it is the only option that makes financial sense. Let me show you why with real numbers and real facts.

## The Price Gap Is Enormous

A brand new slot machine from IGT, Aristocrat, or any major manufacturer costs between $15,000 and $25,000. That is not a typo. The base cabinets start around $15,000, and popular titles with licensed themes can push $25,000 or more.

![IGT S2000 Double Diamond slot machine - Used Slot Shop inventory](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/68753b135742b18163c5ed43.jpeg)

A professionally refurbished version of the same machine, often the exact same model, costs between $800 and $3,500 from our shop. The most popular machines for home use fall in the $1,200 to $2,500 range.

That is an 85 to 95 percent savings for a machine that does the exact same thing. It plays the same games, makes the same sounds, has the same bonus features, and delivers the same experience. The electrons do not know whether the machine is one year old or fifteen years old.

## Why Casinos Replace Perfectly Good Machines

Here is what most people do not understand about the used slot machine market. Casinos do not retire machines because they are broken. They retire them because newer games earn more revenue per square foot of floor space.

A casino floor is real estate. Every square foot needs to generate maximum revenue. When a new game comes out that earns $300 per day compared to an older game earning $200 per day, the older game gets pulled even if it is in perfect working condition. The decision is purely economic.

Most machines are retired after 3 to 7 years on a casino floor. They have been maintained by full-time technicians their entire lives. Casinos employ teams of slot techs who perform preventive maintenance on strict schedules. These machines get better care during their casino life than most cars get from their owners.

When a casino refreshes its floor, hundreds of perfectly functional machines become available to dealers like us. We buy them in bulk, refurbish them, and pass the savings on to home buyers.

## What Professional Refurbishment Includes

Not all refurbished machines are equal. Here is what we do to every machine that comes through our shop before it goes to a customer.

We completely disassemble the cabinet interior. Every component comes out for individual inspection and testing.

The power supply is tested under load. If it does not meet factory voltage specifications on all rails, it gets replaced. Power supplies are the number one failure point on older machines, so we do not take chances here.

All mechanical components are inspected. Hopper motors, coin acceptors, bill validators, reel motors, door hinges, and locks. Anything worn beyond acceptable tolerance gets replaced with new parts.

The main CPU board is tested and the CMOS battery is replaced regardless of remaining life. This ensures years of reliable operation without memory errors.

All lighting is checked and replaced as needed. Bulbs, fluorescent tubes, and LED arrays are verified. Many machines get upgraded from incandescent bulbs to LEDs during refurbishment, which run cooler and last essentially forever.

The bill acceptor gets rebuilt with new belts and cleaned optics, or replaced entirely with a current-generation unit that accepts new bill designs.

The cabinet exterior is cleaned, touched up, and any damaged trim pieces are replaced. The machine should look presentable in your home.

Finally, the machine runs on our test bench for a minimum of 24 hours with automated play cycles to verify no intermittent issues exist.

## What You Sacrifice Buying Used

Let me be honest about what you give up choosing refurbished over new for home use.

You will not have the absolute latest game titles. The newest releases from major manufacturers go exclusively to licensed casinos. You will not find a machine that hit casino floors six months ago on the used market yet. Games typically become available used two to five years after their casino debut.

The cabinet style may look slightly dated. A machine from 2010 has a different aesthetic than one from 2023. The screens may be slightly smaller, the cabinet lines less sleek. For most home users, this is irrelevant because you are after the gaming experience, not the industrial design trophy.

That is genuinely it. The game play is identical. The math is identical. The features work identically. A refurbished machine does not hit differently or pay differently or play differently than it did on day one in the casino.

## What You Do NOT Sacrifice

Game quality. The software running on a refurbished machine is the same software that ran in the casino. The random number generator, the bonus features, the paytables, the animations, the sounds. All identical.

![IGT Game King multi-game video poker machine](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/687a200ece9e185839e98449.jpeg)

Reliability. A properly refurbished machine with new critical components is arguably more reliable than a new machine in its first few months. The burn-in period where infant failures occur happened years ago in the casino. What remains are the components that survived and proved their reliability.

Fun factor. This is the one that matters most. When you sit down at a refurbished Game King and hit four aces on double bonus poker, the excitement is not diminished by the age of the cabinet. The game plays exactly as designed.

## Warranty Comparison

New machines sold to casinos come with a one-year manufacturer warranty. Parts and labor for that first year are covered by IGT, Aristocrat, or whoever made the machine.

Our refurbished machines come with a one-year warranty. We cover parts for any failure that is not caused by physical damage or modification. We also provide free lifetime technical support by phone, which no manufacturer offers to casino operators.

So the warranty coverage is equivalent in duration, and our ongoing tech support actually exceeds what a casino gets from the manufacturer. When your machine has an issue in year three or year five, you can still call us and we will help you fix it at no charge for the support time.

## The Math for Home Use

Let me frame this differently. A new machine at $18,000 versus a refurbished machine at $2,000 leaves you $16,000 ahead. With that savings you could buy seven additional refurbished machines and have a full casino room. Or you could buy one machine, build out an entire home casino with bar, lighting, poker table, and decor, and still have $10,000 in your pocket.

The new machine depreciates catastrophically the moment it enters a private home. It cannot be resold back to a casino. Its resale value on the private market drops to used pricing almost immediately because private buyers will not pay casino pricing.

A refurbished machine holds its value well. A machine purchased from us for $2,000 can typically be resold for $1,200 to $1,500 years later because the used market is liquid and demand is consistent. Your percentage loss on resale is actually lower with refurbished than new.

## Who Should Buy New

New machines make sense for licensed casino operations that need the latest titles to attract players and generate maximum revenue. They make sense for commercial route operators who place machines in bars and restaurants and need the newest games to compete. They do not make sense for home use under any realistic scenario.

If money is truly no object and you want the absolute newest title that just hit Vegas, you will need to go through a licensed distributor and you will pay full price. But understand that you are paying a $15,000 premium for a game that is six months newer than what you could get refurbished.

## Resale Value

Slot machines hold value better than most entertainment purchases. A home theater system from 2015 is worth almost nothing today. A slot machine from 2015 is worth 60 to 75 percent of its refurbished purchase price.

The used slot machine market is active and consistent. People are always entering and exiting the hobby. If you buy a machine from us for $2,200 and decide to sell it five years later, you will likely recover $1,500 or more depending on the model and condition. Compare that to a $2,200 big screen TV that is worth $200 in five years.

Some machines appreciate. Classic titles with cult followings command higher prices as they become scarcer. Original Wheel of Fortune machines from the early 2000s sell for more today than they did ten years ago. Popular titles in limited supply only go up.

## The Bottom Line

For home use, buying a professionally refurbished slot machine over new saves you 85 to 95 percent with virtually no sacrifice in quality, reliability, or enjoyment. You get the same games, the same features, equivalent warranty protection, and better ongoing support.

The only question worth asking is not whether refurbished is worth it. It is which refurbished machine is right for you. Call us and we will help you find the perfect game for your taste and budget from our [334 machines in stock](/shop). Every one of them has been through our full refurbishment process, carries our one-year warranty, and comes with [free lifetime tech support](/warranty). That is a better deal than any casino gets from the manufacturer.
author: Used Slot Shop

title: Buying a Slot Machine as a Gift: The Complete Guide
excerpt: The ultimate guide to gifting a slot machine. Learn who loves them, how to choose the right one, delivery logistics, and what comes in the box.
body:
A slot machine is one of those gifts that creates an immediate reaction. Nobody expects it. Everybody wants to play it. And unlike most big gifts that fade into the background after a few weeks, a slot machine stays entertaining for years. We ship hundreds of machines as gifts every year, and the stories we hear back are fantastic. Let me help you nail this.

## Who Loves a Slot Machine Gift

Retirees are our number one gift recipient. After decades of working, many retirees finally have a game room, spare bedroom, or garage space and the time to enjoy it. A slot machine becomes their daily entertainment and a social magnet when friends visit.

![IGT Game King multi-game video poker machine](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/687a200ece9e185839e98449.jpeg)

Man cave and she shed owners are a close second. If someone has invested in creating their personal space with a bar, sports memorabilia, or entertainment setup, a slot machine is the centerpiece that ties it all together.

Casino enthusiasts who make regular trips to Vegas, Atlantic City, or local casinos absolutely light up when they realize they can play their favorite games at home. No driving, no crowds, no losing real money.

Father's Day and Christmas are our two biggest gift seasons, but we also see a lot of birthday orders, retirement party gifts, and anniversary presents. A 50th birthday slot machine is becoming almost a tradition among a certain crowd.

Couples who enjoy casino trips together often buy machines as an anniversary or Christmas gift to each other. It becomes a shared hobby they enjoy at home between their casino visits.

## How to Choose the Right Machine

The best approach is straightforward: ask what games they play at the casino. If you need to be subtle about it, here are ways to figure it out.

Do they play video poker or reel slots? Video poker players love multi-game machines like the IGT Game King that offers dozens of poker variations. Reel slot players want spinning reels with bonus features.

Do they have a favorite theme? Many casino regulars have a game they always seek out. Buffalo, Double Diamond, Wheel of Fortune, Texas Tea, and Cleopatra are perennially popular titles we stock regularly.

Do they prefer classic simplicity or modern bonus features? Some players love a simple three-reel Double Diamond experience. Others want five reels, free spin bonuses, and pick-em features. Knowing this preference narrows the field considerably.

If you genuinely cannot figure out their preference without spoiling the surprise, a multi-game machine is the safest bet. These offer 50 to 100 different games in one cabinet, virtually guaranteeing something they love is in there.

## Price Ranges for Different Budgets

We have machines at every price point, making this gift accessible whether you are buying from one person or pooling funds with a group.

For $800 to $1,200, you get a solid single-game machine. IGT S-Plus machines with classic titles like Red White and Blue, Double Diamond, or Five Times Pay. These are reliable, attractive, and deliver an authentic casino experience. Perfect when one person is buying the gift.

For $1,500 to $2,500, you step into multi-game machines, video poker platforms like the Game King, newer cabinet styles, and popular themed games. This is our most popular gift range. Many families split this cost among siblings for a parent's retirement or milestone birthday.

For $2,500 to $3,500, you get premium titles, larger cabinets with LCD displays, or top-condition machines with the most sought-after games. These are showpiece gifts that make a statement.

If you are unsure about budget, $1,500 to $2,000 hits the sweet spot of quality, game selection, and impressive presentation.

## How Delivery Works for a Surprise

Keeping a 300-pound machine secret requires some logistics, but we handle this all the time.

Our machines ship on pallets via freight carrier. Standard delivery brings the machine to the curb or driveway. The freight driver will call the delivery contact one day ahead to schedule a delivery window. You can use your own number as the delivery contact to maintain the surprise.

For maximum surprise factor, arrange delivery for the morning of the occasion. Have a friend or family member available to accept delivery while you keep the recipient away from the house. The driver brings it to the driveway, your helper moves it inside with a furniture dolly (we can advise on this), and you reveal it when the moment is right.

Alternatively, we offer scheduling flexibility. Order the machine weeks in advance, pick a specific delivery date, and coordinate accordingly. We hold machines in our warehouse until you give us the green light to ship.

White-glove delivery is available in most metro areas for an additional fee. This means professional movers bring the machine inside to the room of your choice, unbox it, set it up, plug it in, and verify it powers on. This is the ultimate surprise setup because the machine is ready to play the moment they see it.

## Gift Certificates as an Alternative

If logistics make a surprise delivery impossible, or if you want the recipient to choose their own machine, we offer gift certificates in any amount. Here is why some buyers prefer this route.

The recipient gets to choose exactly the game they want. They get the fun of shopping and selecting. And it completely eliminates the risk of choosing a game they would not have picked themselves.

We can provide a physical gift certificate suitable for wrapping if you need something tangible to present on the day. Include a printout of a few machines from our inventory to get them excited about the possibilities.

Many buyers do a hybrid approach: they give the gift certificate on Christmas morning along with a printed photo of one or two machines they were considering, then the recipient calls us after the holidays to make their selection. Best of both worlds.

## What Comes in the Box

![Refurbished IGT S2000 ready to ship from our warehouse](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/6879d05b9227094875c5d253.jpeg)

When your gift arrives, here is everything that is included with every machine we sell.

The machine itself, fully assembled and tested. It is shipped upright and strapped to a pallet with professional packaging and moving blankets to prevent damage.

A complete set of keys. This includes the main door key, the belly door key, the jackpot reset key, and any additional keys specific to the machine model. Usually four to six keys total on a ring.

A power cord appropriate for standard household outlets. Plug it in and it powers right up.

An owner's manual with basic operation instructions, the operator menu settings, and troubleshooting guidance specific to that machine.

For machines that accept coins, we include a starter roll of tokens so they can play immediately without needing to source their own.

The bill acceptor is loaded and functional so they can insert dollar bills and play right away.

Our contact information for [free lifetime tech support](/warranty). Any question, any time, as long as they own the machine.

## Tips for Presentation

A few ideas from customers who have executed memorable slot machine gift reveals.

Some wrap the entire machine in gift wrap. Yes, the shape gives it away at about 5 feet tall and 2 feet wide, but the moment of tearing off that paper is priceless.

Others set up the machine in the game room or garage with a big bow on top and lead the recipient there blindfolded.

A popular approach is handing them a sealed envelope containing a single token and a note that says go look in the garage. They walk in to find the machine lit up in attract mode, ready to accept that first token.

One family rented a storage unit for two weeks before the birthday party, then had the machine delivered to the venue as the final surprise of the evening.

## Ordering Timeline

For holiday gifts, order by early December to guarantee delivery before Christmas. Our standard shipping takes 5 to 10 business days depending on distance from our Kingman, Arizona warehouse.

For birthdays and other specific dates, order at least two weeks ahead. We can coordinate to deliver on a specific date in most cases.

If you are browsing now but not ready to commit, call us and we will note which machines interest you. We can contact you if inventory on a specific title gets low so you do not miss out.

## The Gift That Keeps Giving

Here is what I hear consistently from families who have given a slot machine as a gift. It becomes the activity when people visit. Grandkids line up to pull the handle. Poker night gets a pre-game slot session. The retiree who was getting bored has something to look forward to every day.

A slot machine is not a gift that ends up in a closet. It is a conversation piece, an entertainment center, and a daily source of fun all in one. And with our one-year warranty and lifetime tech support, you are giving ongoing peace of mind along with the machine.

Give us a call and tell us about the person you are shopping for. We will help you pick the perfect machine, coordinate delivery timing, and make sure the surprise lands exactly how you envision it.

title: How to Move a Slot Machine Into Your Home (Without Destroying Your Back or Your Floors)
excerpt: Learn the safe way to move a 150-300 lb slot machine into your home. Weight specs by model, equipment needed, and floor protection tips from a 33-year dealer.
body:
You just bought your first slot machine. It arrives on a pallet at the end of your driveway, and suddenly reality hits: this thing weighs as much as a refrigerator. Now what?

After 33 years of shipping slot machines from our shop in Kingman, Arizona, we have heard every moving horror story in the book. Scratched hardwood floors. Broken casters. A machine that tumbled down three basement steps (the owner was fine, the top glass was not). Every single one of those disasters was preventable.

This guide will walk you through exactly how to move a slot machine safely, whether you are rolling it across a garage floor or hauling it up a flight of stairs to a second-floor game room.

## Know What You Are Lifting: Weight by Model

The first mistake people make is underestimating how heavy these machines are. **Slot machine weight** varies dramatically depending on the era, manufacturer, and cabinet style. Here are the real numbers from machines we handle every day:

- **IGT S2000** (single-game reel machine): 150-175 lbs
- **IGT S2000 Five-Reel**: 165-185 lbs
- **IGT Game King** (multi-game video): 185-210 lbs
- **Bally Alpha** series: 175-200 lbs
- **Aristocrat Viridian**: 200-225 lbs
- **WMS Bluebird 2**: 210-240 lbs
- **Konami Podium**: 195-220 lbs
- **Any machine with a large LCD topper**: Add 25-40 lbs

![IGT S2000 slot machine ready for delivery](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/68753b135742b18163c5ed43.jpeg)

The takeaway: you are dealing with 150-240 lbs of awkward, top-heavy weight in a metal cabinet that was never designed to be carried. Respect that.

## Essential Equipment

Do not attempt to muscle a slot machine with bare hands and good intentions. You need the right equipment, and fortunately none of it is expensive.

### The Must-Haves

- **Appliance dolly (hand truck)**: Not a regular dolly. You need one rated for 600+ lbs with a strap. The strap is critical because slot machines are top-heavy and will tip forward. Budget $40-60 at any hardware store, or rent one from Home Depot for about $20.
- **Furniture sliders**: The felt-bottom discs that go under heavy objects. Get the large 5-inch ones. A pack of four costs about $8 and they will save your floors.
- **Moving blankets**: At least two. One wraps the machine, the other protects doorframes and walls. You can buy a pack of six for $25 at Harbor Freight.
- **Ratchet strap or cam buckle strap**: To secure the machine to the dolly. Do not rely on bungee cords. They stretch, and a 200-lb machine does not care about your bungee cord.

### Nice to Have

- **Plywood sheets (2x4 feet)**: Lay these down to create a rolling path across carpet, grass, or gravel. Two sheets leapfrogged ahead of each other will get you from the driveway to the front door.
- **Rubber stair treads**: If you are going up or down steps, these prevent the dolly wheels from slipping.
- **Work gloves**: Slot machine cabinets have sharp metal edges inside. Wear gloves.

## Reducing the Weight Before You Move

Here is a trick from the shop floor: **you can remove 30-60 lbs from most slot machines** before you move them. Every pound you remove is a pound you do not have to carry.

### Components You Can Safely Remove

- **The top glass or topper assembly**: On most IGT and Bally machines, the top glass or LCD topper lifts out after removing two to four screws or thumb bolts. This can save 10-25 lbs and also protects the most fragile part of the machine.
- **The coin hopper**: Open the main door, unplug the hopper cable, and lift it out. A full hopper can weigh 15-20 lbs. Even empty, it is 5-8 lbs of dead weight.
- **The bill validator (bill acceptor)**: Usually slides out on a rail after releasing a latch. Saves 5-8 lbs and protects a $200 component.
- **Coins or tokens**: If the machine arrived loaded, empty the hopper and any coins in the tray. This alone can be 10-20 lbs.

### What NOT to Remove

- **The CRT or LCD monitor**: These are mounted internally and removing them requires disconnecting wiring harnesses. Not worth the risk of damaging the display or misconnecting wires.
- **The power supply**: Bolted in and wired to everything. Leave it.
- **The motherboard or CPU**: Absolutely do not touch this. One static discharge can damage the **EPROM** chips.

Wrap each removed component in a moving blanket or bubble wrap and transport them separately. When you are ready to reinstall, our [setup guide](/blog/how-to-set-up-your-slot-machine-at-home) walks you through reconnecting everything.

## Protecting Your Floors

Slot machines in casinos sit on commercial carpet or concrete. Your home probably has hardwood, tile, laminate, or residential carpet. All of these can be damaged by a 200-lb machine with metal feet or cheap casters.

### Hard Floors (Hardwood, Tile, Laminate)

- **Never drag a slot machine across a hard floor.** Even six inches of dragging can leave permanent scratches.
- Use furniture sliders under all four corners or feet. Slide, do not roll.
- If the machine has casters, check them first. Casino casters are often flat-spotted, cracked, or have debris embedded in them that will scratch floors. Replace them with soft rubber casters ($15 for a set of four on Amazon) or remove them entirely and use furniture pads.
- Lay down a moving blanket as a runway from the door to the machine's final position.

### Carpet

- Slot machines will crush carpet and pad over time, leaving permanent divots. Place a piece of plywood (painted or stained to look decent) under the machine as a platform.
- For thick carpet, the machine may wobble. The plywood platform eliminates this.

### The Permanent Solution

Once the machine is in its final spot, put it on a **rubber anti-fatigue mat** (the kind used in commercial kitchens). They are about $25 for a 3x5 foot mat, they protect the floor, dampen vibration noise, and give the machine a stable base.

## Moving on a Single Level

If your machine is going from the driveway to a ground-floor room, this is the straightforward scenario.

![IGT S2000 five-reel slot machine](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/6879d05b9227094875c5d253.jpeg)

- Tilt the machine back onto the appliance dolly. Have a second person stabilize the top.
- Strap the machine to the dolly at the midpoint. Tighten until there is zero play.
- Roll across hard, flat surfaces. Use plywood sheets over grass, gravel, or thresholds.
- At doorframes, measure first. Most slot machines are 20-24 inches wide and 20-24 inches deep. Standard interior doors are 30-32 inches wide. You will fit, but remove the door if it is tight. The two minutes spent pulling hinge pins is better than the two hours spent fixing a gouged doorframe.
- When you reach the final spot, lower the machine straight down. Do not let it drop the last two inches.

## Getting a Machine to the Basement

Basements are the most popular spot for home slot machines, and also the most dangerous to access. Basement stairs are typically narrow, steep, and have a low ceiling at the top.

### The Safe Method

- **Remove everything you can** (topper, hopper, bill validator) to get the weight down.
- **Strap the machine to the appliance dolly** with the machine facing you (screen toward you, back of cabinet against the dolly).
- **You go down the stairs first**, walking backward, controlling the dolly. Your helper is at the top, holding the dolly handle and feeding it down to you one step at a time.
- **One step at a time.** Let the dolly wheels bump down to each step. Never let it free-roll.
- **Rest at every third or fourth step.** The person at the bottom is bearing most of the weight. Switch if needed.

> **Pro tip:** If your basement stairs have a turn or landing, stop at the landing, reposition the dolly, and start fresh for the second flight. Trying to navigate a turn on stairs with 150+ lbs is where accidents happen.

### When the Stairs Won't Work

Some basement access is simply too narrow or too steep. In that case, consider a basement window delivery (if the window is large enough) or a Bilco-style bulkhead entrance. We have had customers remove a basement window, build a temporary ramp, and slide the machine down on moving blankets. It works, but measure twice.

## Getting a Machine Upstairs

Moving a slot machine to a second floor is the hardest scenario. You are fighting gravity with an awkward, top-heavy load.

### The Method

- **Two strong people minimum.** Three is better.
- Strap the machine to the dolly, screen facing the stairs (back of machine away from stairs).
- The person at the top pulls the dolly up one step at a time using the handle. The person at the bottom pushes and stabilizes.
- **Never rush.** One step, pause, next step.
- If the machine starts to tip sideways, lower it back down to the last step and reposition. Do not try to save a tip in progress.

### Stairlifts and Motorized Dollies

For machines over 200 lbs going to a second floor, seriously consider renting a **motorized stair-climbing dolly**. Companies like Sunbelt Rentals and United Rentals carry them. They cost $75-150 per day and can safely walk heavy loads up stairs with one operator. Worth every penny for a $2,000 machine.

## When to Hire Professionals

There is no shame in hiring movers. Here are the situations where we strongly recommend it:

- The machine weighs over 200 lbs and needs to go up or down stairs
- You are moving the machine alone (never move a slot machine solo on stairs)
- The path includes tight turns, narrow doorways, or more than 10 steps
- The machine is a high-value piece ($3,000+) and damage would be costly

Hire movers who have experience with heavy equipment or appliances, not just furniture movers. Tell them the exact weight and dimensions. Expect to pay $150-300 for a local in-home move.

## After the Move: Getting Set Up

Once your machine is in position, let it sit for an hour before plugging it in. If it was transported in cold weather, moisture can condense inside the cabinet, and you want that to evaporate before applying power.

Then follow our [setup guide](/blog/how-to-set-up-your-slot-machine-at-home) to get everything connected and running. If you run into any error codes during startup, our [error codes guide](/blog/slot-machine-error-codes-what-they-mean) covers every common code and how to clear it.

And remember, every machine from Used Slot Shop comes with **free lifetime tech support**. If you are standing in your basement staring at a machine that will not power on after the move, call us. We have talked hundreds of customers through post-move issues. That is what we are here for.

Ready to find your machine? [Browse our full inventory](/shop) of over 334 refurbished slot machines, all backed by our [1-year warranty](/warranty) and [nationwide shipping](/shipping).

title: Hosting a Casino Night at Home: How to Use Your Slot Machines for Parties
excerpt: Turn your slot machines into the ultimate party centerpiece. Free play setup, tournament formats, decorating tips, and everything you need for a casino night at home.
body:
There is a moment at every casino night party when it clicks. Someone pulls the handle on a real slot machine, the reels spin, three sevens line up, and the hopper dumps a cascade of tokens into the tray with that unmistakable metallic clatter. The whole room turns. Phones come out. Everyone wants a turn.

That moment is why people buy slot machines from us. Not just for quiet evenings at home (though those are great too), but for nights when you turn your basement, garage, or game room into something nobody expects: a private casino that rivals anything on the Vegas strip.

After 33 years selling and servicing slot machines in Kingman, Arizona, we have helped hundreds of customers set up casino nights. Here is everything we have learned about doing it right.

## Setting Your Machines to Free Play

The single most important thing for a party: **your machines should be in free play mode.** This means guests can play without inserting money, bills, or tokens. They just press the button and spin.

**Free play mode** is available on virtually every machine we sell. It is a setting in the machine's operator menu, not a modification. You are not breaking anything or voiding your warranty. On most IGT machines, you access it through the **service mode** by opening the main door, pressing the service button, and navigating to the operator settings. For a detailed walkthrough, check our [free play guide](/blog/what-is-free-play-mode-on-a-slot-machine).

A few things to know about free play:

- The machine plays exactly like it does with real credits. Same sounds, same animations, same bonus rounds.
- Credits reset when the machine is power-cycled or when the player cashes out (depending on the model).
- You can set a default credit amount, so every guest starts with the same number of credits.
- The **RNG** (random number generator) works identically in free play. The wins are real wins, they just do not pay out real money.

> **Pro tip:** If you want machines to pay out physical tokens for the party atmosphere (that satisfying clatter is half the fun), load the hopper with tokens and leave the machine in standard play mode. Buy a bag of tokens from us for about $20. Guests play with tokens, win tokens, and at the end of the night you collect them all.

## Best Machines for Parties

Not every slot machine is a great party machine. You want games that are visually exciting, easy to understand, and loud. Here are our top picks:

### IGT S2000 (Reel Machines)

The classic. Nothing beats the sound of physical reels spinning and a mechanical hopper paying out coins. Games like **Double Diamond**, **Red White & Blue**, and **Triple Lucky 7s** are instantly recognizable. Even people who have never gambled understand three-reel slots. The S2000 is also our most affordable machine, so you can buy two or three for the price of one video machine.

![IGT S2000 slot machine — the classic party favorite](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/68753b135742b18163c5ed43.jpeg)

### IGT Game King

The ultimate party machine. A single **Game King** cabinet offers 50-80 games in one unit: slots, poker, blackjack, keno. Guests can switch between games without leaving the chair. The video poker alone will keep your card-playing friends busy all night.

![IGT Game King multi-game machine](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/687a200ece9e185839e98449.jpeg)

### Bally and WMS Video Slots

If you want the big bonus rounds with animated sequences and surround sound, these are your machines. Games like **Quick Hit**, **Hot Shot**, and **Monopoly** have bonus features that draw a crowd. When someone triggers a bonus round, expect four or five people to gather around and watch.

## Tournament Formats That Actually Work

Free play is great for casual play, but a **slot tournament** adds structure and competition that keeps energy high all night. Here are three formats we have seen work well:

### Format 1: Timed Sessions

- Each player gets exactly 5 minutes on the machine.
- Everyone starts with the same credit amount (set in the machine's operator menu).
- At the end of 5 minutes, record their credit total on a scoreboard.
- Highest credit total wins.
- This works great with 2-3 machines running simultaneously.

### Format 2: Token Bankroll

- Give each guest 20 tokens at the door.
- They play standard mode (not free play) using their tokens.
- At the end of the night, the person with the most tokens wins.
- This is the most authentic casino experience and adds real stakes (bragging rights, not money).

### Format 3: Bracket Elimination

- Pair guests up, head to head.
- Each pair plays a 3-minute session. Higher credit total advances.
- Single elimination bracket until you have a champion.
- Works best with 8-16 guests and takes about 90 minutes.

For any format, keep a visible scoreboard. A large whiteboard or a TV screen with a spreadsheet works perfectly. The scoreboard creates spectator interest and keeps people engaged even when they are not playing.

## Setting the Scene: Decorating Around Your Machines

Slot machines are already dramatic. A lit-up IGT S2000 in a dim room looks like a piece of Las Vegas. Build on that.

### Lighting

- **Dim the overhead lights.** Slot machines look best when they are the brightest things in the room.
- Add **LED strip lights** in red or purple along the base of walls, under tables, and behind the machines. You can get a 16-foot roll on Amazon for $15.
- If you have a ceiling fan, turn it off. The flickering interaction between fan blades and machine lights is distracting.

### Sound

- Let the machines provide the primary soundscape. The bells, jingles, and coin sounds create authentic atmosphere.
- Add low-volume background music: Rat Pack, jazz, or lounge music. Nothing with lyrics that competes with conversation.
- Do not put a speaker right next to the machines. The machine audio is calibrated for its own speakers.

### Table Setup

- Cover folding tables with black tablecloths. Add a green felt runner down the center for a card-table look.
- Scatter poker chips on tables as decoration. You can get a 300-piece set for $20.
- Print simple rule cards for each machine and place them in acrylic sign holders next to the games.

### The Entrance

- Hang a "Casino Night" banner or marquee sign at the entrance.
- Consider a red carpet runner from the front door to the game room ($15 at any party supply store).
- If you are feeling ambitious, a velvet rope and stanchion at the door sells the whole illusion.

## Food and Drink Stations

Casinos keep players on the floor with free drinks and easy snacks. Apply the same principle.

### Drink Station

- Set up a **self-serve bar** away from the machines. This is important because you do not want drinks near the electronics. A spilled cocktail inside a slot machine is a service call.
- Keep drinks at least 6 feet from any machine.
- Signature cocktails with casino names are a fun touch ("The Jackpot," "The High Roller," "The Double Down").
- Non-alcoholic options are important. Guests who do not drink still want something festive.

### Food

- Finger foods only. Nothing that requires a plate and fork, because guests will be standing around machines.
- Appetizer trays, sliders, wings, bruschetta, and veggie cups work perfectly.
- Set up the food station in a different area than the machines. This creates natural flow and gives guests a reason to circulate.
- **No powdery or greasy foods near the machines.** Cheetos dust on a touchscreen is a nightmare to clean.

## Photography Tips

Slot machines photograph incredibly well, and your guests will want photos.

- **Turn off your flash.** The machine's own lighting creates beautiful, colorful photos. Flash washes it all out.
- The best photo is someone reacting to a win. Have someone ready with a camera during the tournament finals.
- Group photos in front of a lit machine look fantastic. Position people so the machine's top glass is visible above their heads.
- For social media, take a video of someone hitting a bonus round. The combination of lights, sounds, and the player's reaction is pure content.
- Create a hashtag for the party and print it on a small sign next to the machines.

## Logistics: Power, Space, and Noise

### Power Requirements

- Each slot machine draws about 2-3 amps on a standard 120V circuit.
- You can safely run 2-3 machines on a single 15-amp household circuit, but add in other devices (LED lights, speakers, a TV) and you can trip a breaker.
- Know where your breaker panel is before the party. Nothing kills the vibe like every machine going dark at once.
- Use surge protectors, not cheap power strips.

### Space

- Allow at least 3 feet of space in front of each machine for the player's chair and foot room.
- Leave 6 inches behind each machine for ventilation. Slot machines generate heat, especially the older CRT models.
- Budget 2 feet on each side for spectators.
- Total footprint per machine: roughly 4 feet wide by 6 feet deep.

### Noise

- Slot machines are loud. Three machines in a small room can reach conversation-drowning levels.
- Most machines have a volume control in the operator menu. Turn it down to about 60-70% for a party.
- If you have neighbors close by, warn them. The bass from bonus round celebrations can travel through walls, especially in apartments or townhomes.

## After the Party: Maintenance

After a casino night with 20+ guests playing your machines all evening, do a quick maintenance check:

- Wipe down all surfaces with a microfiber cloth. Touchscreens especially.
- Check the coin tray and hopper for foreign objects. We have found bottle caps, olive pits, and once a car key in hoppers after parties.
- Power cycle each machine to clear any accumulated errors.
- If anything seems off, check our [maintenance guide](/blog/slot-machine-maintenance-homeowners-guide) or call our free lifetime tech support line.

## Making It a Tradition

The best casino nights become annual events. We have customers who host Super Bowl casino nights, New Year's Eve parties, and birthday celebrations built around their machines. One customer in Phoenix started a monthly neighborhood poker-and-slots night that has been running for six years.

The machines become more than decoration. They become the reason people come over.

If you are thinking about adding a machine specifically for entertaining, our [buyer's guide](/blog/complete-slot-machine-buyers-guide) covers which models offer the best party experience. And if you are curious about legality in your state, our [state-by-state guide](/blog/is-it-legal-to-own-a-slot-machine-state-by-state-guide) has you covered.

Ready to build your home casino? [Browse over 334 machines](/shop) in our inventory, all with a [1-year warranty](/warranty) and free lifetime tech support. [Contact us](/contact) and we will help you pick the perfect party machines.

title: The Ultimate Slot Machine Glossary: 75+ Terms Every Buyer Should Know
excerpt: From EPROM to TITO, learn 75+ slot machine terms used by dealers, technicians, and collectors. The definitive reference guide for slot machine buyers.
body:
When you start shopping for a slot machine, you run into a wall of jargon. Sellers throw around terms like EPROM, TITO, PAR sheet, and Class II like everyone knows what they mean. Most people do not, and there is no shame in that. These are industry terms from a world that was never designed for consumers.

We have been selling and servicing slot machines in Kingman, Arizona since 1992. In those 33 years, we have explained these terms thousands of times over the phone, in emails, and on our shop floor. This glossary is every one of those explanations collected in one place.

Bookmark this page. You will come back to it.

## A

**Accumulator Bonus** — A bonus feature that builds over time as the player plays. A meter fills or objects collect on screen, eventually triggering a bonus round. Common in newer video slots from Aristocrat and Konami.

**Action Button** — A secondary button on some machines that triggers a feature within a bonus round, such as picking an item or stopping a spinning wheel. Not to be confused with the spin button.

**Ainsworth** — Australian slot machine manufacturer, part of the Novomatic group. Known for titles like Mustang Money and Eagle Bucks. We carry their machines and they hold up well.

**Aristocrat** — Major Australian manufacturer responsible for games like Buffalo, 5 Dragons, and Queen of the Nile. Their **Viridian** and **MARK VI** cabinets are popular with home buyers. Build quality is excellent.

**Aruze** — Japanese manufacturer (now called Universal Entertainment outside Japan). Known for innovative bonus features and the distinctive Innovator cabinet.

**Audit Menu** — A software screen accessible in **service mode** that shows the machine's financial and play history: total coins in, coins out, games played, jackpots hit, and more. Used by casino operators to track performance. On a home machine, it is mostly a curiosity, but it can help diagnose issues.

## B

**Bally** — One of the oldest names in slot machines, now part of Light & Wonder (formerly Scientific Games). Famous for the Quick Hit series, Blazing 7s, and the iconic electromechanical machines of the 1960s-80s. Their Alpha and Pro Series cabinets are popular with home buyers.

![Bally slot machine from Used Slot Shop](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/6879c0e257dd60bea48569a9.jpeg)

**Belly Glass** — The glass panel on the lower front of the machine, below the reel window. It usually displays the game's logo, pay table information, or **denomination**. Belly glass is decorative and interchangeable. Collectors prize rare belly glass panels.

**Bet Max** — A button that automatically wagers the maximum number of credits per spin. On a home machine in **free play mode**, this is the button guests should use to get the full experience with all paylines active.

**Bill Validator (Bill Acceptor)** — The device that accepts paper currency and converts it to credits. Common brands are **JCM** and **MEI/CPI**. On home machines, these can be set to accept bills or disabled entirely. They are one of the most common components to need service.

**Bonus Round** — A special game-within-a-game triggered by specific symbol combinations. Can be free spins, pick-a-prize screens, wheel spins, or elaborate animated sequences. Bonus rounds are what make modern video slots exciting for parties. See our [casino night guide](/blog/hosting-casino-night-at-home-slot-machine-parties) for tips on machines with the best bonus rounds.

## C

**Cabinet** — The physical enclosure that houses all the machine's components. Common cabinet types include **slant-top** (angled screen, player sits) and **upright** (vertical screen, player stands or uses a stool). Upright cabinets are more popular for home use because they take up less floor space.

**Candle** — The light on top of the machine that flashes different colors to signal events: service needed (steady), jackpot (flashing), or specific error codes. Originally used by casino floor attendants. On home machines, it adds to the atmosphere.

**Cash Out (Collect)** — The button that converts credits back to coins, tokens, or a **TITO** ticket. On mechanical hopper machines, pressing this triggers the satisfying cascade of coins.

**Class II** — A legal classification under the Indian Gaming Regulatory Act. **Class II machines** are technically bingo-based: the outcome is determined by a central bingo game, and the slot reels are just a visual representation. Found primarily in tribal casinos. Important for buyers because Class II machines cannot operate independently. They need a bingo server. **Do not buy a Class II machine for home use** unless you confirm it can be converted or run standalone.

**Class III** — The standard slot machine classification. **Class III machines** use a local **RNG** to determine outcomes independently. These are the machines found in Las Vegas and Atlantic City, and they are what you want for home use. Every machine in our [inventory](/shop) is Class III.

**Coin Comparitor** — The device inside the coin acceptor that verifies coins or tokens by checking their metallic composition, weight, and dimensions. Rejects slugs and foreign coins.

**CRT** — Cathode ray tube. The older, bulky monitor technology used in slot machines from the 1990s through mid-2000s. Heavier than LCD screens and generates more heat, but still found in many popular machines like the **IGT Game King**. CRTs can be replaced with LCD conversion kits when they eventually fail.

**Credits** — The unit of play displayed on the machine. One credit equals the machine's **denomination**. A $0.25 machine with 100 credits has $25.00 in play value.

## D

**Denomination** — The value of each credit or coin the machine accepts. Common denominations are $0.01 (penny), $0.05 (nickel), $0.25 (quarter), $0.50 (half dollar), and $1.00. Most home buyers prefer quarter or dollar machines. Multi-denomination machines let you switch between them. Check our [pricing guide](/blog/how-much-does-a-slot-machine-cost-pricing-guide) for how denomination affects machine value.

**Door Open Error** — An error code triggered when the machine's main door, cash door, or top box is not fully closed and latched. The most common error on home machines, usually fixed by simply closing the door firmly until the switch clicks.

## E

**EPROM** — Erasable Programmable Read-Only Memory. A chip that stores the machine's game software and **payout percentage**. In older machines (pre-2005), changing the game or payout percentage required physically swapping the EPROM chip. Newer machines use flash memory or **CompactFlash** cards instead. EPROMs are sensitive to static electricity. Always ground yourself before handling one.

**Error Code** — A numeric or alphanumeric code displayed on the machine's screen or VFD when a problem is detected. Common codes include hopper empty, coin jam, door open, and communication errors. Our [error codes guide](/blog/slot-machine-error-codes-what-they-mean) covers every code you are likely to see and how to clear it.

## F

**Free Play Mode** — A setting that allows the machine to be played without inserting money. The machine automatically adds credits. Available on most machines through the operator menu. Essential for home use and parties. Read our [complete free play guide](/blog/what-is-free-play-mode-on-a-slot-machine) for setup instructions.

**Free Spins** — A bonus feature that awards a set number of spins at no additional cost to the player. Often triggered by scatter symbols. Free spins frequently include multipliers or additional wild symbols.

## G

**Game King** — IGT's multi-game video platform. A single **Game King** cabinet can hold 50-80 different games (slots, poker, blackjack, keno). Extremely popular for home use because of the variety. See our [IGT S2000 guide](/blog/igt-s2000-americas-most-popular-home-slot-machine) for comparison with reel machines.

![IGT Game King multi-game platform](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/687a200ece9e185839e98449.jpeg)

## H

**Handle** — The lever on the side of the machine that spins the reels. Most modern machines use a spin button, but many still include a handle for nostalgia. On mechanical reel machines like the IGT S2000, the handle activates a physical switch that initiates the spin through the same electronics as the button.

**Hit Frequency** — The percentage of spins that result in any win, including pushes (winning back your bet). A machine with a 30% hit frequency pays something on roughly one in three spins. Higher hit frequency does not mean higher payback. It means more frequent, usually smaller, wins.

**Hold Percentage** — The percentage of money the machine keeps over time. The inverse of **payout percentage**. A machine set to 95% payout has a 5% hold. This is how casinos make money. On a home machine, the hold percentage is irrelevant since you are playing with your own money on both sides.

**Hopper** — The mechanical device inside the machine that stores and dispenses coins or tokens. When a player cashes out or hits a paying combination, the hopper motor spins and feeds coins through a chute into the tray. Hoppers jam occasionally and need cleaning. Our [maintenance guide](/blog/slot-machine-maintenance-homeowners-guide) covers hopper care.

## I

**IGT** — International Game Technology, the largest slot machine manufacturer in the world (now part of IGT/Aristocrat after a complex corporate history). Responsible for iconic games and cabinets including the S2000, Game King, Wheel of Fortune, and Double Diamond. The majority of home slot machines in the US are IGT products.

## J

**Jackpot** — The highest-paying combination on the machine. On home machines, a jackpot is exciting but pays from the machine's own hopper (or adds credits in free play). **Progressive jackpots**, which grow with each play, require linking multiple machines or a jackpot controller.

**Jackpot Reset** — A procedure to clear the jackpot display and reset it to the base amount after a jackpot is hit. Done through the operator menu or a RAM clear on most machines.

**JCM** — A major manufacturer of bill validators. If your machine has a bill acceptor, there is a good chance it is a JCM model (iVizion, UBA, WBA). JCM units are reliable and parts are readily available.

## K

**Key Set** — Slot machines use multiple keys for different access levels. A typical machine has keys for the main door (full access), the cash door (hopper and cash box only), the top box, and the CPU compartment. When you buy from us, you receive a complete key set. Keep spares in a safe place.

**Konami** — Japanese manufacturer known for titles like China Shores, Genie's Riches, and Lotus Land. Their Podium and SeleXion cabinets are well-built and popular with collectors.

## L

**LCD** — Liquid Crystal Display. The flat-screen monitor technology used in modern slot machines. Lighter and more energy-efficient than **CRT** monitors. Most machines manufactured after 2008 use LCDs.

**Line (Payline)** — A pattern across the reels that determines winning combinations. Classic three-reel machines have 1-5 paylines. Modern video slots can have 20, 40, 50, or even 243 "ways to win" (which replace traditional paylines).

## M

**Max Bet** — See **Bet Max**.

**Mechanical Reels** — Physical spinning reels with printed symbols, driven by **stepper motors**. Found in classic machines like the IGT S2000. The tactile, audible experience of mechanical reels is why many home buyers prefer them over video slots.

**Metering** — The machine's internal accounting system, accessible through the **audit menu**. Tracks total coin-in, coin-out, games played, and more. Cannot be reset without a **RAM clear** on most machines.

**Multi-Denomination** — A machine that can accept and play at multiple **denomination** levels (e.g., $0.01, $0.05, $0.25, $1.00). The player selects their preferred denomination on screen. Common on video slots.

**Multi-Game** — A machine that offers multiple different games in one cabinet. The **IGT Game King** is the most common example, but Bally, Aristocrat, and WMS also make multi-game platforms.

## N

**Nudge** — A feature in some machines that moves a reel up or down by one position after a spin, potentially turning a near-miss into a win. More common in international (especially UK/Australian) machines than in American slots.

## O

**Operator Menu (Operator Settings)** — The configuration menu accessible by authorized personnel (or the machine owner). Controls payout percentage, denomination, volume, free play settings, and more. Accessed by opening the machine and pressing a service or setup button. The exact method varies by manufacturer.

## P

**PAR Sheet** — Probability Accounting Report. The internal document that details every possible combination on a slot machine, its probability, and its payout. PAR sheets are proprietary and rarely shared publicly. They are the mathematical blueprint of the game. If someone offers to sell you a PAR sheet, it is probably fake.

**Payout Percentage (Return to Player / RTP)** — The theoretical percentage of all money wagered that the machine returns to players over its lifetime. A 95% payout machine returns $95 for every $100 wagered over millions of spins. This is a long-term average, not a guarantee for any individual session. Home machines can typically be set between 85% and 98% via the **EPROM** or operator settings.

**Payline** — See **Line**.

**Pay Table** — The chart showing all winning combinations and their payouts. Displayed on the machine's glass or accessible through a help/info button on video machines. Always check the pay table to understand what the machine pays.

**Progressive Jackpot** — A jackpot that increases with each play, either on a single machine (standalone progressive) or across a group of linked machines (wide-area progressive). Home machines can run standalone progressives. Linking requires a **progressive controller**, which we can provide.

## R

**RAM (Random Access Memory)** — Volatile memory that stores the machine's current state: credits, game in progress, error logs, and metering data. Powered by a battery when the machine is unplugged. If the battery dies, the RAM data is lost.

**RAM Clear** — A procedure that erases the machine's volatile memory and resets it to factory defaults. Clears all metering, error histories, and credits. Required when installing a new EPROM or resolving certain persistent errors. On most IGT machines, it involves setting a specific switch or jumper and powering on. Our [error codes guide](/blog/slot-machine-error-codes-what-they-mean) explains when and how to perform a RAM clear.

**Reel Strip** — The band of symbols printed on a physical reel or mapped to a virtual reel. Each reel strip has a specific number of stops (positions), and the arrangement of symbols on the strip determines the game's odds. On mechanical machines, the reel strips are literal printed ribbons wrapped around the reel cylinder.

**Refurbished** — A used machine that has been professionally restored to full working condition. At Used Slot Shop, our refurbishment process includes cleaning all components, testing every function, replacing worn parts, and installing new batteries. Every refurbished machine in our [inventory](/shop) comes with a [1-year warranty](/warranty).

**RNG (Random Number Generator)** — The computer algorithm inside every modern slot machine that produces random outcomes. The RNG runs continuously, generating thousands of numbers per second. The number generated at the exact instant you press spin determines the outcome. There is no pattern, no cycle, and no way to predict results.

## S

**Scatter Symbol** — A symbol that pays or triggers a feature regardless of its position on the reels (it does not need to land on a specific payline). Typically triggers **free spins** or **bonus rounds** when three or more appear.

**Service Mode** — A diagnostic and configuration mode accessed by opening the machine's door and pressing a service button or key. Allows access to the **operator menu**, **audit menu**, error logs, and test functions. Different from normal gameplay.

**Slant-Top** — A cabinet style where the screen is angled at roughly 30-40 degrees from horizontal. The player sits at a built-in shelf, similar to a desk. Popular in video poker and newer video slots. Takes up more floor space than an **upright** cabinet.

**Stepper Motor** — The precision electric motor that drives each mechanical reel. Stepper motors move in exact increments (steps), allowing the machine's computer to position each reel on a precise symbol. When a stepper motor fails, the reel may not stop accurately or may not spin at all. Replacement motors are available and the swap is straightforward.

**Stop** — A position on a reel where it can land. A typical three-reel mechanical machine has 22 stops per reel. Virtual reels in video slots can have hundreds of stops, which is how they achieve complex odds.

## T

**TITO (Ticket-In, Ticket-Out)** — A cashless system that prints a barcoded ticket instead of dispensing coins. Players insert tickets to add credits or cash out to a ticket. TITO eliminated the need for coin hoppers in most casino machines after 2000. On home machines, TITO printers can be enabled or disabled. Most home buyers prefer the coin hopper experience, but TITO is cleaner and quieter.

**Top Box (Topper)** — The upper section of an upright slot machine, above the main screen. May contain a secondary display, a bonus wheel (like Wheel of Fortune), an LCD topper for animations, or simply a backlit glass panel with the game logo. Top boxes are often removed for home use to reduce the machine's overall height.

**Top Glass** — The backlit glass panel at the top of the machine displaying the game name and graphics. On many machines, this is removable for transport. See our [moving guide](/blog/how-to-move-a-slot-machine-into-your-home) for tips on removing top glass safely.

**Touchscreen** — An LCD screen with a touch-sensitive overlay, allowing players to interact by touching the screen. Common on video slots and multi-game machines. Touchscreens can be recalibrated through the **service mode** if they become inaccurate.

**Tournament Mode** — A special configuration that tracks play for competitive purposes. Some machines have built-in tournament modes that display rankings, time remaining, and scores. More commonly, tournament scoring is done manually with a scoreboard.

## U

**Upright** — A cabinet style where the screen is vertical and the player stands or sits on a separate stool. The most common cabinet type for home use because it takes up the least floor space (roughly 20" x 20" footprint).

## V

**VFD (Vacuum Fluorescent Display)** — The small text display panel, usually green or blue, found on the front of many slot machines. Displays credit count, bet amount, win amount, and error messages. Brighter than LED text and visible in any lighting condition.

**Video Poker** — A poker game played on a slot machine, most commonly five-card draw. The player is dealt five cards, chooses which to hold, and draws replacements. Payouts are based on the resulting poker hand. Available as standalone machines or within multi-game platforms like the **Game King**.

**Video Reels** — Virtual reels displayed on a video screen rather than physical spinning reels. Allows for more complex games with more reels, more stops, and animated features. Most machines manufactured after 2003 use video reels.

**Volatility (Variance)** — A measure of the risk profile of a slot game. **High volatility** games pay less frequently but with larger wins. **Low volatility** games pay more frequently with smaller wins. For home use and parties, low-to-medium volatility games are more fun because players win more often.

## W

**Ways to Win** — An alternative to traditional **paylines**. Instead of specific line patterns, a machine pays for matching symbols on adjacent reels regardless of their vertical position. A 5-reel machine with 3 rows has 243 ways to win (3 x 3 x 3 x 3 x 3). Popularized by Aristocrat's Buffalo and adopted widely.

**Wild Symbol** — A symbol that substitutes for other symbols to complete winning combinations, similar to a wild card in card games. Some wilds have multipliers (2x Wild doubles the win) or are sticky (remain in place for multiple spins).

**WMS (Williams/WMS Gaming)** — Manufacturer known for innovative bonus rounds and licensed themes. Created the Bluebird and Bluebird 2 cabinets and games like Monopoly, Zeus, and Wizard of Oz. Now part of Light & Wonder. WMS machines have some of the most entertaining bonus features for home use.

## Numbers and Symbols

**243 Ways** — See **Ways to Win**.

**3-Reel vs. 5-Reel** — Three-reel machines are the classic style: simpler, faster, and nostalgic. Five-reel machines offer more paylines, more features, and more complex gameplay. Most home buyers start with a three-reel machine and add a five-reel later. Our [buyer's guide](/blog/complete-slot-machine-buyers-guide) helps you decide which is right for you.

**5-Reel** — A machine with five spinning reels (mechanical or video). Allows for more paylines and more complex bonus features than three-reel games. The IGT S2000 five-reel and Bally five-reel uprights are popular choices.

## Quick Reference: Terms You Will See When Shopping

When browsing our [shop](/shop) or any used slot machine listing, here are the terms you will encounter most often:

- **Cabinet type**: Upright or slant-top
- **Denomination**: What coin value the machine plays (quarter, dollar, etc.)
- **Class III**: Standard standalone operation (what you want for home use)
- **Multi-game or single-game**: How many games are in the machine
- **CRT or LCD**: The screen type
- **TITO or coin**: How the machine handles payouts
- **Refurbished**: Professionally restored with tested components
